title: Research Visualizations
text: These figures showcase my analytical workflow for Bayesian prevalence estimation, latent-mixture modelling, and risk factor analysis in veterinary epidemiology. All visualizations are based on simulated data designed to reflect the modelling frameworks used in my published and ongoing research.

title: Research Visualizations
section: 1. Posterior Distribution of True Herd-Level Prevalence
text:
1. Posterior Distribution of True Herd-Level Prevalence









Key takeaway: The posterior distribution shows that the true herd-level prevalence is most plausibly around 14%, with the 95% credible interval reflecting uncertainty after adjustment for imperfect diagnostic performance.

title: Research Visualizations
section: 2. Risk Factors – Forest Plot of Adjusted Odds Ratios
text:
2. Risk Factors – Forest Plot of Adjusted Odds Ratios









Key takeaway: The strongest associations are linked to management and biosecurity practices, with odds ratios above 1 indicating increased odds of herd-level seropositivity.

title: Research Visualizations
section: 3. Apparent vs True Prevalence Comparison
text:
3. Apparent vs True Prevalence Comparison









Key takeaway: Presenting the raw test estimate alongside the Bayesian-adjusted estimate shows how imperfect diagnostic performance can materially underestimate the true herd-level burden.

title: Research Visualizations
section: 4. Simulated Spatiotemporal Prevalence Distribution
text:
4. Simulated Spatiotemporal Prevalence Distribution









Key takeaway: Using a common color scale across survey rounds makes regional heterogeneity easier to compare and highlights persistent higher-burden areas such as Dhaka and Chattogram in this simulated example.

title: Research Visualizations
section: 5. MCMC Trace Plots – Convergence Diagnostics
text:
5. MCMC Trace Plots – Convergence Diagnostics









Key takeaway: The four post-warmup chains fluctuate within the same narrow posterior region without visible drift or persistent separation, suggesting good visual mixing for the prevalence parameter.

title: Research Visualizations
section: 6. Posterior Predictive Check – Model Fit Assessment
text:
6. Posterior Predictive Check – Model Fit Assessment









Key takeaway: The close overlap between the observed and replicated herd-level prevalence distributions suggests that the model reproduces the central tendency, spread, and between-herd heterogeneity of the serological data reasonably well.

title: Research Visualizations
section: 7. Hierarchical Shrinkage – Partial Pooling in Multilevel Models
text:
7. Hierarchical Shrinkage – Partial Pooling in Multilevel Models









Key takeaway: Partial pooling pulls district-specific estimates toward the overall mean, especially for districts with smaller sample sizes, reducing instability and over-interpretation of noisy subgroup estimates.

title: Research Visualizations
section: 8. Model Comparison – Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOO)
text:
8. Model Comparison – Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOO)









Key takeaway: The hierarchical model with diagnostic adjustment shows the strongest expected out-of-sample predictive performance, while simpler models lose predictive accuracy under LOO-CV.

title: Research Visualizations
section: 9. Sensitivity Analysis – Effect of Key Assumptions on the Prevalence Estimate
text:
9. Sensitivity Analysis – Effect of Key Assumptions on the Prevalence Estimate









Key takeaway: In this simulated one-way sensitivity analysis, the posterior median prevalence appears most sensitive to plausible changes in diagnostic specificity, whereas comparable changes in sensitivity and prior mean produce smaller shifts in the estimated prevalence.

title: Research Visualizations
section: 10. ROC-like Curve – Diagnostic Test Performance Evaluation
text:
10. ROC-like Curve – Diagnostic Test Performance Evaluation









Key takeaway: This simulated ROC curve suggests that the diagnostic score has strong discriminatory ability, with a clear sensitivity–specificity trade-off across thresholds; however, the figure should be interpreted as an illustration of test performance rather than validation from observed field data.

title: Research Visualizations
section: 11. Mixture Model Components – Latent Subpopulation Decomposition
text:
11. Mixture Model Components – Latent Subpopulation Decomposition









Key takeaway: This simulated finite-mixture plot illustrates how an apparently single assay-signal distribution may reflect multiple overlapping latent subgroups, which can be useful for modelling heterogeneity and improving inference when population-level measurements are not well represented by a single distribution.

title: Research Visualizations
section: 12. Bayesian Gaussian Mixture Illustration – Class Separation and Decision Thresholds
text:
12. Bayesian Gaussian Mixture Illustration – Class Separation and Decision Thresholds
Illustrative latent-class distributions with threshold-based separation









Key takeaway: This illustrative Bayesian Gaussian mixture figure shows how an observed assay-signal distribution can be represented as overlapping latent subgroups, with threshold-based cutoffs providing a practical framework for class separation under measurement uncertainty.

title: Teaching
text:
Teaching and Work Experience
This page will contain my teaching, mentoring, and professional experience.

title: Research Projects
text:
I am passionate about applying advanced epidemiological and statistical tools to tackle zoonotic and infectious diseases at the human–animal–environment interface, with a focus on dairy cattle health in low-resource settings. My work integrates field diagnostics, Bayesian modeling, and One Health principles to generate actionable insights for disease control, antimicrobial stewardship, and sustainable livestock production. As an early-career researcher from Bangladesh Agricultural University, I am eager to collaborate with international teams to scale these approaches globally.


Nov 2023 – Present
This ongoing project quantifies the true burden and transmission dynamics of priority zoonotic pathogens in intensive dairy systems — a critical yet understudied context in South Asia. Focus pathogens include brucellosis, paratuberculosis (Johne’s disease), bovine viral diarrhea (BVD), neosporosis, and Q fever (Coxiella burnetii). We use bulk milk screening and molecular diagnostics to address diagnostic imperfections and surveillance gaps.
Key contributions and outputs: - Estimated herd- and cow-level true prevalence using indirect ELISA and real-time PCR on bulk milk samples. - Identified farm-level risk factors (management practices, biosecurity, environmental exposures) via multivariable and Bayesian approaches. - Performed multiplex qPCR on fetal membranes from abortion cases to detect Brucella spp., Coxiella burnetii, Neospora caninum, Leptospira spp., and others. - Presented findings at the BSVER Annual Scientific Conference (2024). - Contributing to multiple manuscripts in high-impact journals (e.g., PLOS ONE, Veterinary Sciences).
This work bridges local impact (improved herd health for Bangladeshi smallholders) with global relevance (zoonotic risk in emerging dairy economies).
Skills & Methods: Indirect ELISA · Real-time qPCR · DNA extraction · Bayesian prevalence estimation · Risk factor analysis · Scientific visualization (R, BioRender, Adobe Illustrator) · Zoonotic disease surveillance · Dairy herd health monitoring



Jan 2025 – Present
Supervisor: Prof. Dr. A.K.M. Anisur Rahman
This project develops cost-effective, nutritionally optimized TMR formulations using locally sourced feeds to enhance growth performance, health, and welfare in Bangladeshi beef cattle — addressing feed scarcity and sustainability in tropical smallholder systems.
Key contributions: - Assisted in designing and executing controlled feeding trials with on-farm monitoring. - Applied laboratory diagnostics (ELISA, PCR) and statistical modeling (R) to evaluate impacts on growth metrics, immune response, and disease resistance. - Collaborated on practical recommendations for farmers, integrating veterinary medicine with food security and rural livelihoods.
This initiative demonstrates scalable solutions for climate-resilient livestock production in resource-limited regions.
Skills & Methods: Veterinary nutrition · Experimental trial design · Statistical analysis (R) · Laboratory techniques (ELISA, PCR) · Field data collection · One Health applications in livestock production



Self-initiated research program (leading a team of 10 collaborators)
This program addresses critical gaps in the global understanding of human brucellosis — a neglected zoonosis with massive underestimation due to imperfect diagnostics, heterogeneous surveillance, and non-specific symptoms. It comprises three interconnected, independently publishable studies:

Bayesian test-adjusted meta-analysis — Synthesizing global seroprevalence while propagating uncertainty in diagnostic sensitivity/specificity (no perfect gold standard) to derive credible true prevalence estimates across regions.
Bayesian spatiotemporal disease mapping — Inferring global hotspots, trends, and clusters under incomplete reporting, borrowing strength across space/time to overcome data sparsity.
One Health ecological driver modeling — Quantifying environmental (climate, humidity), livestock density, and socioeconomic drivers while addressing spatial confounding and ecological inference biases.

This builds on my expertise in Bayesian latent-class models and zoonotic epidemiology, with strong potential for high-impact outputs and international collaboration (diagnostics, spatial epi, One Health groups).
Skills & Methods: Systematic review · Bayesian hierarchical modeling (latent class, spatiotemporal, ecological inference) · Advanced R programming · Geospatial analysis (QGIS) · One Health integration · Uncertainty propagation in diagnostics

title: Why I Moved Beyond Apparent Prevalence in Veterinary Epidemiology
text:
March 19, 2026
When I first started working with prevalence data, I used the same approach that many of us learn early on: count the number of positive test results and divide by the total number tested. It is simple, quick, and easy to explain. In many papers, that is still exactly what gets reported.
At first, that felt perfectly reasonable to me.
Over time, though, I became less comfortable with it. The more I read about diagnostic test performance, and the more I worked with serological data from clustered animal populations, the more I felt that the usual prevalence calculation was not telling the full story.
What we usually observe is apparent prevalence. What we usually care about is true prevalence. Those are not always the same thing.

title: Why I Moved Beyond Apparent Prevalence in Veterinary Epidemiology
section: Why that difference started to matter to me
text:
Why that difference started to matter to me
In veterinary epidemiology, we often work with imperfect diagnostics. Sensitivity is not perfect. Specificity is not perfect. In some settings, that may not change the interpretation very much. But in low-prevalence populations, even a small amount of misclassification can matter a lot.
A modest loss of specificity can make prevalence look higher than it really is. A modest loss of sensitivity can hide infected animals that should have been counted. Once the data are clustered within herds, farms, or regions, the problem becomes harder to dismiss as a minor technical detail.
That was the point when apparent prevalence started to feel more like a rough first summary than a final answer.

title: Why I Moved Beyond Apparent Prevalence in Veterinary Epidemiology
section: What Bayesian modelling changed for me
text:
What Bayesian modelling changed for me
The main attraction of Bayesian modelling was not complexity for its own sake. It was that the framework allowed me to handle uncertainty more honestly.
Instead of treating test performance as fixed and perfect, I could treat sensitivity and specificity as uncertain quantities informed by validation studies or previous literature. Instead of reporting one corrected number, I could estimate a full posterior distribution for prevalence and show the uncertainty directly. Instead of ignoring herd structure, I could model the clustering that actually exists in field data.
That shift changed the way I thought about the problem. I stopped seeing prevalence estimation as a simple calculation and started seeing it as an inference problem under imperfect measurement.
That has been much more aligned with the kind of data I actually work with.

title: Why I Moved Beyond Apparent Prevalence in Veterinary Epidemiology
section: The key idea
text:
The key idea
The important step is to distinguish between the latent infection status and the observed test result.
In plain terms, the model asks two separate questions:
First, what is the probability that an animal is truly infected?
Second, given that true state, what is the probability that the diagnostic test returns a positive result?
That separation is what allows imperfect sensitivity and specificity to enter the model in a principled way. Without that step, we are still modelling observed positivity, not true prevalence.

title: Why I Moved Beyond Apparent Prevalence in Veterinary Epidemiology
section: Why I still think this is worth the extra effort
text:
Why I still think this is worth the extra effort
Bayesian models take longer to fit. They require stronger justification. Priors need to be explained. Convergence has to be checked. Posterior predictive assessment and sensitivity analysis are part of the job, not optional extras.
So yes, there is more work involved.
But for me, the trade-off has been worth it. The estimates are more defensible. The uncertainty is clearer. The modelling assumptions are easier to state explicitly. And the final interpretation is much closer to the real epidemiologic question I want to answer.
In low-resource veterinary settings, where diagnostics are rarely perfect and field data are often clustered, I do not think this is just a statistical preference. In many cases, it is the more responsible way to analyse prevalence.
That is why I moved beyond apparent prevalence.
Not because the traditional approach is useless, but because I wanted my estimates to reflect the uncertainty that is already there, whether we acknowledge it or not.
Several of the visualizations on this site are simplified illustrations of that modelling logic, built from simulated data for transparency while reflecting the analytical frameworks used in my research.

title: About
text:
I am a veterinary researcher and graduate student at the Department of Medicine, Faculty of Veterinary Science, Bangladesh Agricultural University. My academic work focuses on veterinary epidemiology, infectious diseases in livestock, and One Health research, with an emphasis on understanding disease patterns in animal populations and generating evidence that can inform animal and public health decision-making.
I work under the supervision of Prof. Dr. A.K.M. Anisur Rahman in the Epidemiology and Preventive Medicine Laboratory, where I have been involved in research activities for the past three years. During this time, I have participated in several epidemiological studies on infectious diseases in livestock populations, gaining experience in field data collection, laboratory diagnostics, and epidemiological analysis.
My research interests center on epidemiological approaches to infectious diseases, including disease prevalence estimation, risk factor analysis, and evidence synthesis. I am particularly interested in applying quantitative and data-driven methods to better understand the dynamics of livestock diseases and their implications for food systems and global health.


My primary research interests include:

Veterinary epidemiology

Infectious diseases in livestock

One Health and zoonotic diseases

Systematic review and meta-analysis

Epidemiological study design and risk factor analysis

Quantitative data analysis in health research




My long-term goal is to contribute to high-quality research in infectious disease epidemiology and global animal health. I aim to collaborate with international researchers and pursue advanced academic training that will allow me to develop stronger expertise in epidemiological modeling, evidence synthesis, and data-driven health research.

title: Md. Nazmul Islam
text:
Md Nazmul Islam


MS Student in Medicine



 Bangladesh


 Bangladesh Agricultural University


 Email


 LinkedIn


 GitHub


 Google Scholar


 ResearchGate


 ORCID





Welcome to my personal website.


I am a Doctor of Veterinary Medicine (DVM) graduate and an MS candidate in Medicine at the Department of Medicine, Bangladesh Agricultural University.


My research focuses on veterinary epidemiology, zoonotic infectious diseases in livestock, and One Health approaches, integrating Bayesian statistical modeling, field diagnostics, and epidemiological risk analysis to support improved disease surveillance and control in dairy production systems.


Working under the supervision of A.K.M. Anisur Rahman, I combine practical field and laboratory experience with advanced analytical tools such as R for Bayesian analysis, ELISA/qPCR diagnostics, and QGIS-based spatial analysis to produce robust, reproducible epidemiological evidence.


You can explore my publications, working papers, research projects, public engagement activities, and teaching contributions by navigating the sections of this website.


Key Highlights



Co-first author publications in PLOS ONE and Veterinary Sciences, with several additional manuscripts currently under peer review in international journals.


Conducted Bayesian prevalence estimation and risk-factor studies on brucellosis, paratuberculosis, Q fever, bovine viral diarrhea, and subclinical mastitis in Bangladeshi dairy herds.


Leading an independent global epidemiology project on human brucellosis, applying Bayesian meta-synthesis, spatiotemporal mapping, and One Health frameworks with a multidisciplinary research team.


Recipient of the National Science and Technology (NST) Fellowship 2026.



Research Interests



Veterinary epidemiology and zoonotic disease modeling


Bayesian hierarchical and latent-class statistical methods


Dairy herd health and infectious disease surveillance


One Health approaches in low- and middle-income settings


Systematic reviews, meta-analysis, and geospatial epidemiology

title: Publications
text:
Here you will find my peer-reviewed journal articles, manuscripts under review, conference proceedings, and research presentations. My work focuses on veterinary epidemiology, infectious disease modelling, Bayesian prevalence estimation, risk factor analysis, and production-impact assessment in dairy cattle and other ruminant populations in Bangladesh.
My research applies Bayesian latent-class models, hierarchical and mixed-effects regression, multinomial disease-state modelling, mixture models, and applied epidemiological statistics to investigate zoonotic and production-limiting diseases, including brucellosis, paratuberculosis, bovine viral diarrhoea, Coxiella burnetii, subclinical mastitis, and fascioliasis.
I have contributed as first author, equally contributing first author, or major analytical contributor across several studies, with roles spanning study design, field sampling, laboratory diagnostics, data curation, statistical analysis, Bayesian modelling, visualisation, and manuscript preparation.

title: Timeline
text:
Personal timeline containing significant events in my life in reverse chronological order (last updated March, 2026).




Equally Contributing First Author – PLOS One
Published second Q1 paper: “Estimated true prevalence and associated risk factors of bovine paratuberculosis antibodies in dairy herds in Bangladesh”. Applied Bayesian methods to adjust for diagnostic imperfections and identify farm-level drivers in South Asian intensive dairy systems.
January 2026




National Science and Technology (NST) Fellowship
Awarded competitive research fellowship by the Ministry of Science and Technology, Government of Bangladesh, to support advanced epidemiological studies in livestock zoonoses.
January 2026




Oral Presentation – IOCVS 2025
Presented “Feasibility of On-Farm Somatic Cell Count Screening for Subclinical Mastitis: Prevalence and Risk Factors in a Commercial Dairy Herd in Bangladesh” at the 1st International Online Conference on Veterinary Science (IOCVS), 4 December 2025.
December 2025




Equally Contributing First Author – Q1 Journal
Published first lead-author paper: “Prevalence and Risk Factors of Bovine Viral Diarrhea Virus Antibodies in Dairy Herds of Bangladesh”. Combined field sampling, serology, and multivariable risk factor analysis.
August 2025




Aspire Leaders Program – Finalist & Alumni
Selected among 6,175 finalists from more than 36,000 applicants for the 2025 Aspire Leaders Program. Completed 40 hours of intensive training in leadership, critical thinking, global impact strategy, and communication.
August 2025




Admission – MS in Medicine
Enrolled in Master of Science in Medicine, Department of Medicine, Faculty of Veterinary Science, Bangladesh Agricultural University.
April 2025




Published Book – Amazon Kindle
Authored and self-published The AI Prompt Handbook: Master Your Skills and Accelerate Growth. Practical guide on using large language models for academic writing, research productivity, and personal development.
April 2025




Graduated – Doctor of Veterinary Medicine (DVM)
Completed DVM degree from Bangladesh Agricultural University. Received comprehensive training in veterinary clinical practice, epidemiology, pathology, and public health.
January 2025




Veterinary Internship
Completed a comprehensive veterinary internship across multiple clinical and research settings, including government hospitals, diagnostic laboratories, dairy farms, military farms, and private clinics. Performed clinical examinations, treatments, surgical assistance, and field consultations under professional supervision.
November 2024




Research Assistant
Joined the Epidemiology and Preventive Medicine Laboratory, Department of Medicine, Bangladesh Agricultural University. Conducted more than 3 years of full-time research on zoonotic pathogens in dairy cattle, including brucellosis, paratuberculosis, BVD, and Q fever, with focus on field diagnostics, Bayesian prevalence estimation, and risk factor modeling.
January 2023




Founder – EduTricks Academy
Launched a free online educational platform through Facebook page and group to provide academic support, study strategies, and subject tutorials for secondary and higher-secondary students across Bangladesh. Reached more than 1,000 learners.
January 2022




Undergraduate Studies – DVM
Started my Doctor of Veterinary Medicine (DVM) degree at Bangladesh Agricultural University.
January 2019




Merit Scholarship – Higher Secondary Certificate (HSC)
Awarded merit-based scholarship by the Board of Intermediate and Secondary Education, Dinajpur, Bangladesh, for outstanding performance in HSC examinations.
February 2018




Merit Scholarship – Secondary School Certificate (SSC)
Awarded merit-based scholarship by the Board of Intermediate and Secondary Education, Dinajpur, Bangladesh, for exceptional results in SSC examinations.
June 2016
